title: Annular Solar Eclipse
visible_in: Annularity is visible only from Antarctica. A partial eclipse is visible from parts of southern Africa and the far southern tip of South America, plus surrounding Southern Ocean regions.
description: An annular solar eclipse happens when the Moon covers the Sun but appears slightly smaller, leaving a bright “ring of fire.” Use ISO 12312-2 eclipse glasses (or certified solar filters) for ALL partial/annular phases. Never look at the Sun without proper protection.

title: Total Lunar Eclipse
visible_in: Visible across much of eastern Asia and Australia/Pacific, and across the Americas (where the Moon is above the horizon).
description: A total lunar eclipse occurs when Earth’s shadow fully covers the Moon, often turning it copper-red (“blood moon”). Safe to view with the naked eye. Binoculars or a small telescope can enhance color and shadow detail near maximum.

title: Total Solar Eclipse
visible_in: Totality crosses the Arctic region including Greenland and Iceland, then reaches the Iberian region late in the day (Portugal/northern Spain). Partial eclipse visible across much of Europe and parts of Africa and North America.
description: A total solar eclipse turns day into twilight inside the narrow path of totality. Use certified solar filters for all partial phases; only remove filters briefly during totality if you are inside the totality path. In parts of Spain/Portugal the Sun is low, so a clear horizon is important.

title: Partial Lunar Eclipse
visible_in: Visible across the eastern Pacific and the Americas, with visibility extending into Europe and Africa (where the Moon is above the horizon).
description: During a partial lunar eclipse, part of the Moon moves into Earth’s dark umbral shadow. The shaded portion may appear gray to reddish near maximum. Safe for naked-eye viewing; binoculars help reveal the shadow edge and subtle color gradients.

title: Annular Solar Eclipse
visible_in: Annularity crosses parts of southern South America and continues toward West Africa near sunset. Partial eclipse visible across a wider surrounding region.
description: An annular eclipse produces a bright ring when the Moon doesn’t fully cover the Sun. Solar protection is required at all times—annularity is never safe to view without filters. Best views are from inside the narrow annular path.

title: Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
visible_in: Visible across much of Europe and Africa, plus parts of the Americas and Asia (where the Moon is above the horizon).
description: A penumbral lunar eclipse is subtle: the Moon passes through Earth’s outer shadow, causing gentle shading rather than a dramatic bite. This one is relatively deep for a penumbral eclipse, so dimming may be noticeable near maximum. Safe for naked-eye viewing.

title: Penumbral Lunar Eclipse (Extremely Slight)
visible_in: Visible mainly from eastern Africa, Asia, Australia and the Pacific night-side (where the Moon is above the horizon).
description: This is an extremely slight penumbral eclipse and may be hard to notice visually. Any shading is likely faint and brief near maximum. Best results come from careful side-by-side photos taken before and at maximum. Safe for naked-eye viewing.

title: Total Solar Eclipse
visible_in: Totality crosses the Strait of Gibraltar region and continues across North Africa into the Middle East (including parts of Spain/Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, and near Somalia). Partial eclipse visible across much of Europe and Africa.
description: A major total solar eclipse with an unusually long maximum totality (over 6 minutes near the centerline). Outside the totality path, it is only partial. Use certified solar filters for all partial phases; remove filters only during totality and only if you are inside the totality path.

title: Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
visible_in: Visible primarily across the Pacific and the Americas night-side (where the Moon is above the horizon).
description: A moderate penumbral eclipse where gentle shading may be visible on the Moon near maximum. The effect is subtle compared with partial/total lunar eclipses, but easier than extremely slight penumbral events. Safe to observe with the naked eye; photos can help reveal the gradient.
